a conception that matters primarily to members of “society” as the term is used in the

phrase “society pages.”

And just that is what we see in continental privacy law: a high-status conception

of privacy, a “society” conception of privacy. In fact, it is almost comical to read off the

names in the captions of the leading post-war continental cases. Open a book on

comparative privacy law; here are the names you will see: Princess Soraya, 70 Princess

Caroline of Monaco, 71 Prince Ernst August of Hanover. 72 There is a remarkable

disproportion of royalty in continental privacy thinking. Down to this day, in fact,

German texts list royalty first among the classes of “public figures” who require special

70
   In Donald Kommers, The Constitutional Jurisprudence of the Federal Republic of Germany, 2d ed.
(Durham: Duke U. Press, 1997), 124-128.
71
   NJW 96, 1128; most recently BVerfG, Urt. V. 15.12.1999, 1 BvR 653/96, in NJW 2000 1021.
72
   For his peculiar importance, Soehring, Presserecht, 428, with further citations.

23
 treatment in the law of privacy73 ; while French texts, the product of a deeper democratic

tradition, only list royalty second, after politicians. 74 “Members of the aristocracy”75 too

are presented as classes that must be specially treated. These are textbooks written in

worlds that remain very different from ours. Even the non-royals and non-aristocrats

involved in the leading European privacy cases are often very prominent persons indeed:

Hjalmar Schacht, former Nazi finance minister, Robert Barcia, long-time eminence grise

of the French Trotskyite party. 76 Indeed, an American cynic who wants to mock the

vaunted continental commitment to privacy will point gleefully at these names. At core,

the American will sneer, the continental protection for privacy grants everyone alike the

right to be safe from paparazzi. Does this really have anything to do with the values of a

true democracy? At best, continental privacy law is, not a form of protection for

universal human “personhood,” but a means of regulating the relations between

celebrities and the rest of us.

Nevertheless, as I want to insist, to take that mocking attitude would be to

underestimate the moral claims of European leveling up, as it expresses itself in privacy

law. There is more to the law than its practical impact. The law also aims to express

social values—the continental law of privacy as much as or more than any other body of

law. What the continental law of privacy expresses is the fundamental social importance

of a commitment to extend royal treatment to everyone. Indeed, we cannot understand

our transatlantic conflicts if we do not recognize the authentically wide social extension
